text: Lecture	  58	  –	  EEG	  and	  Regulation	  of	  Cortical	  Excitability	   	   Objectives	  	   • Review	  concept	  of	  ongoing	  activity	  in	  human	  cortex	   • How	  is	  it	  measured(EEG)?	   • How	  and	  where	  are	  EEG	  rhythms	  generated?	   • Variation	  with	  level	  of	  arousal	   • Roles	  of	  rhythms	  in	  cognition	  etc.	   • How	  can	  EEG	  measurements	  be	  used	  clinically?	   	   Overview	   • There	  is	  constant	  ongoing	  electrical	  activity	  in	  the	  cortex	   • Measured	  using	  an	  electroencephalograph	  (EEG)	   • Measured	  in	  mV	  (because	  the	  signals	  have	  to	  travel	  through	  the	  skull)	   	   Features	  of	  EEG	   • synchronous	  α	  waves	  (8-­‐13Hz)	  are	  more	  in	  parietal	  and	  occipital	  lobe	  	  Relaxed	  brain	   • Desynchronized:	  small	  high	  freq.	  waves	  	  Active	  brain	   • Patterns	  vary	  with	  arousal	   o γ	  -­‐	  Desynchronised	  small	  high	  frequency	  waves	  (associated	  with	  high	  cognitive/attentional	  states)	   o α	  -­‐	  medium	  amplitude	  and	  frequency	  (associated	  with	  awake	  relaxed	  eyes	  closed/drowsy	  state)	   o Theta	  etc	  –	  large	  amplitude,	  low	  freq	  (2Hz)	   o δ	  -­‐	  large	  amplitude,	  low	  frequency	  (associated	  with	  deep	  sleep)	   o β	  -­‐	  small	  amplitude,	  high	  frequency	  (associated	  with	  dreaming,	  REM	  sleep,	  similar	  to	  alert	  brain)	   	   Generation	   • Not	  APs	  (as	  these	  are	  too	  small	  to	  detect)	   • Arise	  from	  synaptic	  potentials	  in	  organised	  populations	  of	  cortical	   neurons	  that	  summate	  in	  a	  slow	  fashion	   • Cortical	  potentials	  comes	  from	  underlying	  thalamus	  in	  reticular	  nucleus	   	   Why	  rhythmic	   1. Cortical	  thalamic	  loops	  that	  are	  self	  inhibitory	   a. Reverberatory	  circuits	  	  -­‐	  establish	  rhythmic	  firing	  for	  rhythmic	   functions	  eg	  sleep	   b. These	  involve	  specific	  and	  non-­‐specific	  thalamic	  neurons	   2. Intrinsic	  rhythmic	  firing	  of	  thalamic	  neurons	   a. Can	  switch	  from	  bursting	  to	  continuous	  firing	  depending	  on	  alert	   state	   b. There	  are	  subthalamic	  neurons	  that	  feed	  input	  to	  non-­‐specific	  and	   specific	  nuclei	  of	  thalamus	  (and	  cortex)	  that	  cause	  the	  change	  in	  it’s	  firing	  rate	  	  Ascending	   reticular	  activating	  system	   i. Barbiturate	  anaesthetics	  suppress	  ARAS	  to	  induce	  sleep.	   	   Coma	   • Permanent	  state	  of	  sleep	  in	  which	  the	  cortex	  can	  not	  be	  aroused	   	   Causes	  of	  coma	   • Wide	  spread	  cortical	  damage	  eg	  ischemia	  or	  trauma	   • Damage	  to	  ARAS	   	   Functions	  of	  cortical	  rhythms	   • Regulation	  state	  of	  arousal	  and	  sleep	  waking	  cycle	   • Prime	  cortical	  circuitry	  for	  cognitive	  tasks	   o Slower	  waves	  why	  is	  this?	  (he	  said	  when	  you	  are	  slow	  waves	  there	  is	  less	  processing	  and	  response	   to	  sensory	  input	  –	  is	  this	  what	  your	  point	  is	  related	  to?)	   • Role	  in	  learning	  and	  memory	  circuits	   o Slow	  waves	  during	  sleep	  help	  consolidate	  learning	  due	  to	  synaptic	  changes.	   • Binding	  of	  stimuli	  (from	  different	  cortical	  areas)	  to	  create	  a	  unified	  perception	  of	  the	  world	  –	  generates	   conscious	  awareness.	   	   	   EEG	  Patterns	   • Normal	  –	  smooth	  and	  rhythmic	   • α	   • β	   • Theta	   • δ	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Abnormal	  –	  associated	  with	  seizures	  or	  cortical	  damage	   • Seizure	  waves	   • Convulsions	   • Sharp	  waves	   	   Diagnosis	   • Patterns	  are	  characteristic	  of	  various	  disorders	   o Epilepsy	  and	  lesions	   o Sleep	  abnormalities	   o Coma	  and	  prognosis	   o Brain	  death	   o Psychiatric	  disorders	   	   Summary	  	   • electrical	  activity	  of	  cortex	  measured	  with	  surface	  electrodes	  –	  EEG	   • state	  of	  consciousness	  depends	  on	  the	  pattern	  and	  level	  of	  cortical	  activity	  	   • overall	  neural	  activity	  of	  cerebral	  cortex	  regulated	  by	  subcortical	  brains	  structures	  (thalamus,	  ARAS)	  	   • coma	  can	  result	  from	  widespread	  damage	  to	  cortex	  or	  damage	  to	  subcortical	  structures	  	   • EEG	  has	  wide	  range	  of	  clinical	  uses
